symptoms.symptom_name: Continuous severe RUQ pain >6 hours radiating to the right shoulder tip
symptoms.onset: about 8 hours ago after a fatty meal
symptoms.character: constant, deep, boring; worse on inspiration

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Continuous RUQ pain >6 hours with referred right-shoulder pain is characteristic; duration discriminates from colic.
Simple colic is self-limiting (minutes to a few hours); sustained pain argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Prior brief self-resolving episodes of similar pain after fatty meals

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A history of post-prandial biliary colic indicates pre-existing gallstone disease, the substrate for cholecystitis.
Established cholelithiasis is the precursor lesion; the new sustained febrile episode is the progression.
Fever signals secondary bacterial inflammation of the obstructed gallbladder and is part of the systemic-inflammation TG18 limb.
Fever + RUQ pain is two-thirds of Charcot's triad; jaundice would complete it.
Colic is afebrile; fever moves the diagnosis to an inflammatory/infective process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe nausea with three episodes of bilious vomiting; cannot tolerate oral fluids

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nausea and vomiting commonly accompany biliary inflammation; intolerance of oral intake mandates hospital IV support and nil-by-mouth.
Vomiting is non-specific and also fits pancreatitis, which must be excluded with lipase.
Fatty food triggers cholecystokinin-driven gallbladder contraction against an obstructing stone, a classic precipitant.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain worse on deep inspiration with shallow splinted breathing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Inspiratory worsening reflects diaphragmatic/peritoneal irritation over the inflamed gallbladder — the history correlate of a positive Murphy's sign.
Pleuritic-type inspiratory pain can also occur in RLL pneumonia, kept in view until excluded.

symptoms.symptom_name: Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on deep subcostal palpation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A positive Murphy's sign is highly specific for acute cholecystitis; it is a clinician-elicited sign the AI Doctor cannot obtain and is sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Localised RUQ guarding and rigidity on palpation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Localised peritonism over the gallbladder; clinician-only palpation finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia HR 104 with fever, BP 130/82, RR 20, SpO2 98% (clinician-measured vitals)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Tachycardia proportionate to fever supports systemic inflammation; measured vitals are a sealed clinician observation — the AI infers systemic upset from reported fever/rigors, not a measured pulse/BP.
Jaundice with fever and RUQ pain completes Charcot's triad; a subtle exam finding requiring adequate light, sealed as clinician-only.

symptoms.symptom_name: Transabdominal ultrasound: gallbladder wall >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign

investigation_details.investigation_name: Transabdominal ultrasound of the biliary tree
investigation_details.expected_result: Gallbladder wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallstones and a positive sonographic Murphy's sign
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Diagnosis and severity of acute cholecystitis cannot be confirmed and complications (empyema, perforation) may be missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid and a sonographic Murphy's sign are the imaging hallmark; hospital-only, never available in telehealth.

investigation_details.expected_result: Normal or mildly raised (used to exclude concurrent acute pancreatitis)
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Concurrent gallstone pancreatitis could be missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lipase >3x upper limit of normal would indicate concurrent pancreatitis; needed to complete the picture.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory markers and LFTs (FBC/WCC, CRP, bilirubin, ALP, GGT)

investigation_details.investigation_name: Full blood count, CRP and liver function tests
investigation_details.expected_result: Leucocytosis and raised CRP; deranged LFTs (raised ALP/GGT/bilirubin) would suggest CBD obstruction/cholangitis
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Severity grading and detection of biliary obstruction/cholangitis are not possible.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Leucocytosis and raised CRP satisfy the systemic-inflammation limb of TG18; abnormal LFTs point toward cholangitis.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Continuous RUQ pain >6 h + fever + positive Murphy's sign + sonographic gallbladder-wall thickening is the classic diagnostic cluster; the AI Doctor can access the first two by history but must recognise the pattern and escalate for the clinician-only signs and imaging.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Transabdominal ultrasound of the biliary tree
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line imaging to confirm acute cholecystitis and detect complications; arranged via ED, not obtainable in telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Bloods: FBC, CRP, LFTs, serum lipase, U&E
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirm systemic inflammation, exclude concurrent pancreatitis, detect biliary obstruction/cholangitis and assess hydration; hospital-obtained.
Febrile biliary sepsis — cultures before antibiotics guide therapy; hospital-obtained.

Ultrasound is the appropriate first-line biliary investigation; CT is second-line if ultrasound is inconclusive or complications are suspected, and is a hospital decision — the AI Doctor should not direct primary CT.
